data.title: DXP Enterprises: Organic Growth Outpaces M&A, Margins Improve (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In February, after a weak Q3 report, I recommended tactically holding stocks and waiting for the release of the Q4 report, as there were concerns that the report might be weaker than expected. However, DXP Enterprises, Inc. (DXPE) was pleased with the advance of the consensus forecast for both revenue and EPS. Against the background of a good report, the stock has grown even more, and since my Buy recommendation in October last year, the total growth has been about 27%. The strategy chosen by the management to diversify the business and reduce dependence on oil and gas makes revenue more stable and more predictable. At the same time, due to the growth of financial indicators, the multiples have decreased slightly, and for many of them, the company's shares are now trading at a discount to the sectoral median values. Considering the financial situation and the current multiplier rating, I am upgrading the rating from Hold to Buy. According to my estimate, by building a DCF model, the fair value of the shares is $185 in the baseline scenario with an upside of 14.5% to the current price ($161.7 at the time of writing).

Strong Quarter Driven by Organic Growth and IPS Expansion

The latest key event in the company's business is related to the release of the Q4 financial statements and the market's reaction to the demonstrated results. I liked the results of the published report. The company managed to beat market expectations in terms of revenue and EPS. I would like to analyze key aspects of the reporting so that further forecasts for Q1 from the next chapter (the report is due out in May) are more understandable to the reader. But before that, I recommend that you read my first two articles about the company, where I have analyzed in detail all the company's activities, its business model, and its development strategy.

Revenue for the quarter increased by 12% yoy to $527.4 million. For comparison, I predicted about $500 million due to fewer working days and a seasonal slowdown in business by the end of the year, and the consensus forecast was $499 million, which is almost 6% lower than the actual result. Compared to Q3, revenue increased by 2.7%. An important caveat is that revenue from the core business grew strongly this quarter, and only part of the revenue was added by the absorbed companies. Organic revenue increased by as much as 16% to $505.5 million, and additional revenue from new assets even decreased by $13 million. This indicates a high demand for the company's solutions, as evidenced by the growth of sales per business day increased by 12% yoy to $8.5 million.

The situation has not changed by segment for several consecutive quarters—two key segments are growing at double-digit rates, while the third continues to stagnate. Revenue at Service Centers increased by 14.6% yoy to $356.2 million. In the 10-K, management points to high demand and the growth of large orders for adhesives in some regions, as well as growing interest in metalworking and air compressors. IPS revenue grew even faster, by 12.7%, to $110.0 million. IPS is more marginal, and high revenue growth rates help to increase the overall marginality of the business. According to management, the segment's revenue increased mainly due to new large client projects, as well as continued diversification into water and wastewater. But SCS sales have not grown, which has been attributed to lower activity from oil and gas customers for several quarters in a row. For investors, it is more important that the first two segments grow, and if IPS is growing faster than Service Centers, then this is doubly good, since the operating margin is growing along with it.

That's what happened in the end—operating profit increased by 18.8% yoy to $46.7 million. Profit growth faster than revenue resulted in an improvement in the operating margin from 8.3% to 8.9%. The adjusted EBITDA margin also increased to 11.2% from 10.7%. The IPS segment became the main engine of growth, where the operating margin increased from 15.6% to 18.0%. Operating expenses grew in Q4, but higher sales growth at Service Centers and IPS helped boost overall margins. The situation turned out to be slightly lower at the net profit level, but due to a one-time strong increase in the effective tax rate from 6% to 26%. As a result, the net margin decreased to 4.3% from 4.5%. Adjusted EPS rose from $1.29 to $1.39, with a consensus forecast of $1.30. I was close in my forecast, expecting $1.40 EPS. EPS growth was marginally impacted by share buybacks, but otherwise all the growth was due to last year's weak results and strong operating results in Q4’2026.

Overall, the company's report turned out to be strong, except for a nuance related to a strong increase in tax expenses. But even with this in mind, all indicators have grown at a faster pace, primarily due to organic business growth, not acquisitions. If DXP continues at the same pace, the stock will inevitably rise by another 10-20%.

Consensus and Management Aligned on Growth Outlook

Management does not make forecasts, either quarterly or yearly. From the management's comments, it can be concluded that they expect both organic growth in both business segments and acquisition-driven growth to continue. The situation with oil and gas is also expected to improve in the second half of the year. In the 10-K, the company writes that it expects growth rates similar to those in 2025, but subject to maintaining a favorable macroeconomic environment. Interest expenses are also expected to rise, as the company needs financing to maintain its growth rate. This will put pressure on net income, but it will reduce tax expenses.

The market consensus for the full year 2026 assumes revenue growth of 10.20% yoy to $2.22 billion and EPS of 12.92% yoy to $6.12. From the first quarter, the market expects revenue growth of 11.53% yoy to $531.5 million and EPS growth of 2.38% yoy to $1.29. Market expectations generally agree with management's comments. According to my expectations, the company will deliver a strong first quarter due to high demand in the two largest segments. I expect the following results for Q1: revenue of $532-535 million and EPS of $1.28-1.30.

Conservative DCF Still Shows Upside

Let's move on to the main thing, namely the assessment. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030), and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted, which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

I have slightly revised the assumptions embedded in the DCF model towards greater conservatism. I forecast the revenue CAGR for 2026-2030 at 6% versus 12.6% for 2021-2025. Likewise, I expect that the revenue growth rate will gradually slow down with the saturation of the market and the gradual normalization of demand. The company will reach maturity by 2028-2029 and will no longer focus on growth but on maintaining the achieved market share and monetization. Besides, the company probably won't be able to grow revenue faster without new M&A deals. This will help increase the operating margin from 8.86% to 10.76% by 2030. Cost control will significantly affect the growth of the operating margin, but still, high competition will not allow it to grow faster. Capital expenditures will be kept in the range of 1.5-2% to maintain high business growth rates. Thus, the FCF yield for 2026-2030 will be about 6.20% versus 3.88% for 2021-2025. In fact, according to my forecast, from 2028 the company will focus not on quantity but on quality, trying to increase the marginality of the business and free cash flow.

I got the WACC for the company at 7.56% due to a beta of 0.95x, an equity risk premium (taken from Damodaran's table) of 4.3% for the USA, and a 4.34% risk-free rate, which I took as 10-year USA Government Bond Yields. The cost of debt was 6.5%. I use the latest TTM interest expense divided by the latest one-year quarterly average debt to get the simplified cost of debt.

Due to the growth of financial indicators, the multiples have decreased slightly. For many of them, the company's shares are currently trading at a discount to the sectoral medians. Stocks with parameters like those of DXP usually trade at large premiums to the market. I think the multiples will rise in the near future, especially if the company releases a strong Q1 report.

The DCF model unequivocally confirms my judgments. In the most negative scenario (with WACC rising and multiples falling), the fair value of the shares should be higher by 0-7% compared to the current market price. That is, there is no downside even in the worst-case scenario. In the basic scenario, the stock should be 11-15% more expensive, and in the positive scenario, it should be 22% more expensive. I upgrade the rating from Hold to Buy with a target price of $185 and an upside of 14.5%.

Moderate Buybacks with Focus on Balance Sheet

The debt burden on the business remains elevated due to frequent M&A transactions. By the end of 2025, the company's total debt amounted to $924.5 million, with cash in the accounts in the region of $303.8 million. This results in a net debt of $620.7 million. The debt/EBITDA ratio is only 4.13x, and the net debt/EBITDA is 2.74x. Operating profit exceeds interest expenses by almost 3 times. The company does not pay dividends, investing its free funds in business development and maintaining growth rates. DXP is holding buybacks, but in 2025 significantly fewer shares were bought back—only 0.2 million shares worth $17.0 million. Most likely, management is trying to buy back shares only on drawdowns.

Risks

The company's risks have not changed much since the last article. DXP is most dependent on a favorable macroeconomic environment, as recessions and stagnation in the economy lead to a reduction in orders and aggregate demand. DXP conducts a lot of M&A transactions to support growth, and this creates additional risks associated with the integration of purchased companies, overpaying for low-cost, and inflated expectations of synergy with new businesses. The other risks are standard, such as high competition, oil price volatility, and a high debt burden.

Final Thoughts

DXP is in a good financial position, and high demand allows for rapid growth in revenue and operating margins. High oil prices have also been playing into the hands of businesses since March. I expect the company to release a strong report for Q1, which will create additional interest in the stock, and its price will rise. That's why I'm upgrading the rating from Hold to Buy.

data.title: Dominion Energy: A Discounted Utility With A Premium Yield
data.description:
Dominion (D) will be reporting earnings on May 1, and right now is a good time to revisit the story, which continues to steadily power along. The stock screens attractively on both comparative multiple valuation and its dividend yield of 4.2%. With a clear capital plan stretching out to 2030, there is real visibility into the company's future growth potential and delivery of the EPS growth algorithm.

Business Profile

Dominion is a regulated utility primarily operating across Virginia and South Carolina. As a regulated utility, returns are much more stable than a standard operating business. The company is 95% state-regulated on its utility operations and has had a 10.0% consolidated rate base CAGR. That CAGR has actually stepped up from 7.5% just two years ago because of data center demand for electricity causing prices to go up. The company's simple strategy is to provide reliable and affordable service to customers.

As a result, the company has a very clear algorithm:

  • 5-7% annual EPS growth rate
  • ~4% dividend yield
  • 9-11% total shareholder return

Recently, with the onset of AI and data center demand increasing the price for electricity, you're looking at a more variable component to the top line than in prior years. 2023 through 2026 has seen heightened PJM prices. The PJM is the Pennsylvania-New Jersey-Maryland regional transmission organization (RTO) that dictates the movement of electricity across 13 states, including Washington, DC. This is the area in which Dominion operates.

Investors can actually monitor live PJM prices, as well as load, among other things like outages, and gain a better understanding of how to think about the base rate growth for Dominion. It should remain steady, albeit at a 10% annual growth from here.

The company has a clear capital plan through 2030. The point of this, quite simply, is to grow the rate base. As they grow the rate base, the more capital they have available to return to shareholders and fund even more projects in the future. The plan totals $64.7 billion, and it is largely concentrated with a $54.8 billion capital plan for Dominion Energy in Virginia and $7.6 billion in South Carolina. There are residual capital outlays for Contracted Energy and Corporate. The company will be focused on investing in grid reliability.

The company's capital plan is comprised of 63% core grid and gas infrastructure, 25% electric transmission and distribution, with the balance of 18% being gas generation. The balance is solar, nuclear, and offshore wind. There is clear visibility for rate growth into the end of the decade.

To double click on the wind exposure the company has, we turn to the Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind (CVOW) project. The first delivery of electricity in this project is in the current quarter—Q1 2026. The project is still under partial construction and will not be completed until early 2027. This project carries a capital budget of $11.5 billion and requires $1.2 billion of additional funding from here. The next steps for the project are the installation of transition pieces, cable installation, installation, and commissioning of the wind turbine generators, and other transmission-related activities.

With a diversified capital plan for the next five years, investors can have confidence in both dividend safety and that the algorithm will be hit.

Financial Profile and Comparative Valuation

To build a forward estimate profile for Dominion, we need to start with the base rate trajectory. Virginia and South Carolina have lower electricity costs than the national average. Dominion notes the national average is around 17.8 c/kWh, while their business in Virginia charges 17.1 c/kWh and their business in South Carolina charges 15.7 c/kWh. This is favorable because it means there is room for electricity base rates to grow towards the national average before they brush up against affordability issues. As I said before, this is compounding at a 10% growth rate per year.

From there, we can start to think about squaring their guidance. The guidance this year calls for 2026 operating EPS to be $3.45-$3.69 (midpoint $3.57). Dominion does not provide top-line or other expense guidance.

Street is looking for $4.26 billion in revenue in Q1 and a small beat versus Street at $4.30 billion. The company has been beating top-line frequently, primarily driven by data center demand. The largest cost buckets for the quarter are electric fuel and other energy-related purchases, as well as other operations and maintenance. Assuming stable costs, albeit rising for electric, I am holding margins steady into Q1. Flowing through the revenue beat, then, I arrive at $0.90 versus the street at $0.86 in EPS.

Dominion is continuing to post a strong track record in EPS beats, having not missed since 2023. The consistency should drive confidence for investors that this company can close its valuation gap to peers.

That prompts a comparative valuation analysis. I'm using the peer set of Duke Energy (DUK), Southern Company (SO), American Electric Power (AEP), and NextEra Energy (NEE).

  • NEE trades at 22.82x forward earnings and 15.6x forward EV/EBITDA.
  • DUK trades at 19.08x forward earnings and 11.5x forward EV/EBITDA
  • SO trades at 20.48x forward earnings and 12.8x forward EV/EBITDA
  • AEP trades at 20.93x forward earnings and 12.47x forward EV/EBITDA

These peers are relative to D trading at 17.33x forward earnings and 13x forward EV/EBITDA.

  • NEE EBIT margin 30.23% with EPS growth of 8%
  • DUK EBIT margin 26.98% with EPS growth of 5-7%
  • SO EBIT margin 26.72% with EPS growth of near 6%
  • AEP EBIT margin 25.25% with EPS growth of 7-9%

These are relative to D's EBIT margin at 32.88% and 5-7% earnings growth guidance. It's interesting to see a discounted multiple for Dominion despite better operating margins to a significant degree and similar earnings growth versus peers. The spreads between these companies should be much tighter, and the discount doesn't seem to be warranted.

Dominion has been steadily grinding higher since late 2023, just after the time the Fed Funds rate hit its peak for the cycle. As a reminder, utility stocks are heavily influenced by interest rates. The stock maintains an upward trend, positioned above its 200-day moving average, and based upon the comparative analysis I just outlined, there really should be scope for outsized capital appreciation versus peers as the discount is unwarranted.

I also find it interesting to look at Dominion on a dividend basis versus these peers. Lower yields in this space can attract discounted multiples. D presently yields 4.3%. This is relative to NEE at 2.7%, DUK at 3.33%, SO at 3.2%, and AEP at 2.85%. At nearly a point better yield than the closest competitor, it is very surprising to see this valuation disconnect. While there are still scars from Dominion having cut their dividend back in 2020, the trust concerns should be well behind the company, as considerable time has passed.

While the yield for Dominion is at a lower level than the past few years, it is still best in class versus peers.

Risks

Given the nature of regulatory utilities, upside is effectively capped. Trying to argue for large inflections in base rates or dramatic amounts of upside is simply not feasible. State regulators focus on required returns on equity in order to make sure consumers are not overcharged for the basic necessity that is electricity.

Utilities like Dominion are also highly sensitive to interest rates given large capital outlays and leveraged balance sheets. As rates come down, utility stocks can outperform, and vice versa. Stubbornly high rates could force multiple compressions. Interest costs, in particular, can hurt bottom-line earnings. Leverage for most businesses is somewhere in the 0-2x net debt/adjusted EBITDA range, but businesses in the utility sector can often have 4-5x net leverage and comfortably operate. Financial conditions pressure is a clear risk. Finally, while companies like Dominion have great visibility on future projects, these projects can go over budget and force more spending, which likely means more balance sheet pressure as financing costs spike.

Conclusion

Dominion has a clear trajectory for further growth with a stable capital plan through 2030 that should provide solid base rate growth, driving decent earnings growth and stable dividend growth. What's most surprising about this stock is the fact that it trades at a discount to its peer group but has better margins, a better dividend yield, and similar earnings growth, all the while maintaining the safety of a regulated utility. There is a clear opportunity for multiple upsides on this name to close the gap.

data.title: The Nightview Fund Q1 2026 - The Software Opportunity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Nightview Fund Q1 2026 Investor Letter.

The Software Opportunity: Too Much Fear, Not Enough Thinking

Over the past several months, a legitimate debate has emerged about AI business models.

If the cost of training and running AI falls dramatically — if “intelligence becomes a commodity,” as some have declared — then what happens to the companies that have been charging for it? This is a reasonable question.

The market’s answer, when that fear took hold, was to sell everything software-adjacent and ask questions later.

We think that reaction was both understandable and wrong for a reason that is easy to miss: the best software companies are not primarily selling AI. They are selling workflows, integrations, data networks, and the organizational infrastructure that modern enterprises cannot function without. AI makes these businesses more valuable, not less, because it accelerates the rate at which their platforms become indispensable.

Large enterprises have little incentive to rebuild software infrastructure for non-core functions, and the bar for displacing an entrenched incumbent is extraordinarily high. Switching costs, compliance requirements, and organizational inertia all conspire against it. Disruption will happen at the margins, but for the truly embedded software businesses, AI is less a threat than potentially a tailwind.

Axon Enterprise (AXON)

Axon is not a software company in the way most people use that term, which is precisely why we think it is one of the most underappreciated businesses in our portfolio.

It is the operating system of public safety in America and, increasingly, around the world. The company sells Tasers, body cameras, in-car cameras, and — this is the key — a cloud-based evidence management platform called Axon Evidence, which has become the de facto system of record for law enforcement agencies across the country.

The software business alone is impressive: high recurring revenue, long contract durations, minimal churn, and a customer base — police departments and government agencies — that, once converted, essentially never leaves. The hardware serves as a distribution mechanism for the software. As Axon expands into records management, computer-aided dispatch, and AI-powered video analysis, the addressable market expands dramatically.

We initiated our position in Axon in late February as the stock corrected sharply in the broader software selloff. At the prices we paid, we were acquiring a business with roughly 30% annual revenue growth, expanding margins, and a near-monopoly position in its core market — for a valuation that reflected significant investor skepticism.

ServiceNow (NOW)

If you want to understand why we are bullish on enterprise software despite the AI-disruption narrative, ServiceNow is the clearest expression of our thesis.

The platform is, in the most literal sense, the workflow engine of large enterprises. It manages IT service requests, HR processes, legal requests, facilities management, and, increasingly, the cross-departmental orchestration that large organizations require to function. Roughly 85% of the Fortune 500 uses ServiceNow in some capacity. The average customer has expanded their usage year over year, a pattern that has persisted for years running.

Our view is that AI does not threaten this business so much as it accelerates it.

ServiceNow has been embedding AI capabilities — including agentic automation that can resolve IT tickets without human intervention — directly into its platform. Each AI feature gives customers more reason to deepen their reliance on the platform. The result is a flywheel: more AI features drive more workflow automation, which drives more data into ServiceNow’s systems, which makes the AI smarter, which drives more expansion.

We added ServiceNow to the portfolio in mid-March after the stock had sold off more than 20% from its highs on fears that AI would enable customers to build their own workflow tools. We find this thesis unpersuasive. The switching costs are enormous, the ecosystem deep, and the platform increasingly central to how enterprises operate. The selloff was, in our view, a gift.

Shopify (SHOP)

Shopify is a business that the market has consistently struggled to value, and for understandable reasons: it does not fit neatly into a single category. It is part payments company, part software platform, part logistics network, and part financial services provider. The revenue mix has evolved steadily.

Margins compress and expand as the company invests in new capabilities. For investors who want a clean, simple model, Shopify is frustrating. For investors who can look past the quarterly noise and see what the business is actually building, it remains exciting.

The core thesis is simple: Shopify is becoming a commerce infrastructure layer for the modern internet. When a merchant starts a Shopify store, they are plugging into a system that handles payments, inventory management, fulfillment logistics, customer data, marketing automation, and increasingly, cross-border commerce. The platform is deeply sticky.

The AI story at Shopify is also compelling. The company has been building AI-powered tools for merchants — product descriptions, ad copy, customer service automation, pricing optimization — that are genuinely useful, not just marketing. As AI capabilities become part of the core platform, Shopify becomes more valuable to more merchants, which expands the addressable market and deepens the moat. We added Shopify to the portfolio in mid-March and see it as a long-term compounder.

Autodesk (ADSK)

Autodesk is the kind of business that gets less credit than it deserves because it is not flashy. It makes software for architects, engineers, and construction professionals. It has been in the business for over forty years. It is not, on the surface, a headline-grabbing story. But underneath the surface, Autodesk is one of the most durable software franchises in existence, and its transition over the past several years from a perpetual-license model to a subscription-based cloud platform has dramatically improved the predictability and quality of its earnings.

We initiated our Autodesk position in late February when the stock was trading at a meaningful discount to our estimate of intrinsic value, driven by broader software sector weakness. The subscription transition is essentially complete. Free cash flow is growing. The company is beginning to layer AI capabilities — particularly in generative design and automated compliance checking — that should drive further expansion in average revenue per user. This is a classic Nightview holding: durable competitive moat, recurring revenue, and a long compounding runway.

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the NITE ETF.
